segments.title: Mikasa as a living museum ship
segments.summary: On board Mikasa, Clarke reflects on a museum ship kept lively by visitors, welcoming staff, and interest from Japanese and U.S. Navy guests.
segments.body: Clarke presents the visit to Mikasa as more than a photography stop: the ship is full of people across age groups on consecutive days. He singles out the staff for making the visit welcoming, then notes conversations with Japanese visitors and personnel from the U.S. Seventh Fleet. That mix makes the vessel feel like a public place of naval memory rather than a static exhibit. Open this moment for a brief sense of how a preserved warship can remain socially active and internationally meaningful.

evidence.note: Clarke describes the visitors, staff, and reception aboard Mikasa.
